comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around technical insights and personal experiences related to JPEG compression and image formats. Participants share knowledge about frequency decomposition, comparisons with other compression methods, and preferences for newer image formats like WebP and AVIF.

comments.author: danwills
comments.text: Having played a bit with Discrete FFT (with FFTW on 2D images in a Shake plugin we made at work ages ago) makes the DCT coefficients make so much more sense! I really wonder whether the frequency-decomposition could happen at multiple scale levels though? Sounds slightly like wavelets and maybe that's how jpeg2000 works?.. Yeah I looked it up, uses DWT so it kinda sounds like it! Shame it hasn't taken off so far!? Or maybe there's an even better way?
comments.analysis: This comment shows a solid understanding of frequency domain techniques and correctly connects JPEG compression to wavelet-based methods like JPEG2000, reflecting accurate and insightful knowledge.

comments.author: tomalaci
comments.text:
I usually have a script/alias cmd to automatically convert images to webp. The webp format has pretty much replaced jpg/jpeg (lacks transparency/alpha support) and png (no compression) formats for me.

There is also AVIF format which is newer and better but it needs to still mature a bit with better support/compatability.

If you are hosting images it is nice to use avif and fallback to webp.
comments.analysis: This comment provides practical advice on image format usage and correctly notes the advantages and limitations of JPEG, WebP, and AVIF formats, reflecting current trends in image compression.

comments.author: tmilard
comments.text: Thanks for the sharing :  I now understood more how sampling of image works. And going from RGB to lunimesence+chroma works. interesting and usefull
comments.analysis: This comment expresses gratitude and indicates a basic understanding of color space transformation in image compression, though it contains a minor typo and lacks technical depth.

comments.author: bambax
comments.text:
So the idea behind JPEG is the same as behind MP3: we filter out what we don't perceive.

I wonder if other species would look at our images or listen to our sounds and register with horror all the gaping holes everywhere.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately summarizes the perceptual basis of lossy compression but adds a speculative and humorous remark about other species, blending fact with lighthearted conjecture.

comments.author: NooneAtAll3
comments.text:

seems like website doesn't work without webgl enabled... why?
comments.analysis: This comment reports a technical issue related to website functionality dependent on WebGL, which is plausible but not directly related to JPEG compression discussion.
